content:
# Exodus Chapter 4 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~33r–33v (PDF pp. 64–65); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 44–45 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_004_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_004_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_004_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_004_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And Moses answered and said, "But behold, they will not believe me and will not listen to my voice, for they will say, 'The LORD did not appear to you.'"

**2** And the LORD said to him, "What is that in your hand?" And he said, "A staff."

**3** And He said, "Cast it on the ground." And he cast it on the ground, and it became a serpent, and Moses fled from before it.

**4** And the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand and seize it by its tail" -- and he stretched out his hand and took hold of it, and it became a staff in his palm --

**5** "so that they may believe that the LORD, the God of their fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, appeared to you."

**6** And the LORD said to him further, "Now put your hand into your bosom." And he put his hand into his bosom, and he brought it out, and behold, his hand was leprous, like snow.

**7** And He said, "Put your hand back into your bosom." And he put his hand back into his bosom, and he brought it out from his bosom, and behold, it had returned to be like his flesh.

**8** "And it shall be, if they will not believe you and will not heed the voice of the first sign, then they will believe the voice of the latter sign.

**9** And it shall be, if they will not believe even these two signs and will not listen to your voice, then you shall take from the water of the Nile and pour it on the dry ground, and the water that you take from the Nile shall become blood on the dry ground."

**10** And Moses said to the LORD, "Please, my Lord, I am not a man of words, neither from yesterday nor from the day before nor from the time You have spoken to Your servant, for I am heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue."

**11** And the LORD said to him, "Who placed a mouth for man? Or who makes him mute, or deaf, or seeing, or blind? Is it not I, the LORD?

**12** And now, go, and I Myself will be with your mouth, and I will instruct you what you shall speak."

**13** And he said, "Please, my Lord, send, I pray, by the hand of whomever You will send."

**14** And the anger of the LORD burned against Moses, and He said, "Is there not Aaron your brother, the Levite? I know that he can surely speak. And also, behold, he is coming out to meet you, and he will see you and will rejoice in his heart.

**15** And you shall speak to him and put the words in his mouth, and I Myself will be with your mouth and with his mouth, and I will instruct you what you shall do.

**16** And he shall speak for you to the people, and it shall be that he shall be for you as a mouth, and you shall be for him as God.

**17** And this staff you shall take in your hand, with which you shall do the signs."

**18** And Moses went and returned to Jethro his father-in-law and said to him, "Let me go, I pray, and return to my brothers who are in Egypt, that I may see whether they are still alive." And Jethro said to Moses, "Go in peace."

**19** And the LORD said to Moses in Midian, "Go, return to Egypt, for all the men who were seeking your life have died."

**20** And Moses took his wife and his sons and set them upon the donkey, and he returned to the land of Egypt. And Moses took the staff of God in his hand.

**21** And the LORD said to Moses, "When you go to return to Egypt, see all the wonders that I have put in your hand, and you shall do them before Pharaoh. But I will harden his heart, and he will not send the people out.

**22** And you shall say to Pharaoh, 'Thus says the LORD: Israel is My son, My firstborn.

**23** And I say to you, send My son out that he may serve Me. And if you refuse to send him out, behold, I am about to kill your son, your firstborn.'"

**24** And it was on the way, at the lodging place, that the LORD met him and sought to kill him.

**25** And Zipporah took a flint and cut off the foreskin of her son and touched it to his feet, and she said, "For you are a bridegroom of blood to me."

**26** And He relented from him. Then she said, "A bridegroom of blood," on account of the circumcision.

**27** And the LORD said to Aaron, "Go to meet Moses in the wilderness." And he went and met him at the mountain of God and kissed him.

**28** And Moses told Aaron all the words of the LORD with which He had sent him, and all the signs with which He had commanded him.

**29** And Moses and Aaron went and gathered all the elders of the sons of Israel.

**30** And Aaron spoke all the words that the LORD had spoken to Moses, and he did the signs before the eyes of the people.

**31** And the people believed. And they heard that the LORD had attended to the sons of Israel and that He had seen their affliction. And they bowed down and worshipped.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folio ~33r (PDF p. 64) contains the opening of Exodus 4, continuing from the end of chapter 3 on the same page. The text flows across the three-column layout with clear vocalization and cantillation marks. The chapter continues onto folio ~33v (PDF p. 65), completing through verse 31 and transitioning into chapter 5. The Masorah parva annotations are visible in the margins throughout. The Codex Vaticanus (pp. 44-45) was consulted as a secondary witness; the Greek text of Exodus 4 begins partway through page 44 (marked CAP. XLIX) and continues through page 45 (where CAP. L begins, marking the transition into Exodus 5). The uncial majuscule script runs continuously without word division.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"A staff" (v. 2):** Hebrew matteh. The same word used for a tribal "staff" or "rod" of authority. When God takes it up in verse 20, it becomes "the staff of God" (matteh ha'Elohim), marking its transformation from a shepherd's tool to an instrument of divine power. LOGOS maintains the single term "staff" throughout.

2. **"A serpent" (v. 3):** Hebrew nachash, the common word for serpent (the same word used in Genesis 3:1). The LXX reads drakon ("serpent/dragon"). In Exodus 7:9-10, a different word is used (tannin, "sea creature" or "great serpent") for the sign performed before Pharaoh. LOGOS preserves this distinction between nachash here and tannin in chapter 7.

3. **"Leprous, like snow" (v. 6):** Hebrew metsora'at kashaleg. The term tsara'at encompasses a range of skin conditions beyond modern leprosy (Hansen's disease). LOGOS retains the traditional rendering "leprous" as a formal equivalent, with the simile "like snow" indicating the whiteness of the affliction. The LXX reads hosei chion ("like snow"), confirming the comparison.

4. **"The voice of the first sign... the latter sign" (v. 8):** Hebrew qol ha'ot harishon... ha'ot ha'acharon. Signs have a "voice" (qol) -- they speak, they testify. The Hebrew personifies the miraculous acts as having a communicative dimension. LOGOS preserves this striking idiom rather than smoothing to "evidence of the sign" or "message of the sign."

5. **"Heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue" (v. 10):** Hebrew kevad peh ukhvad lashon. Moses describes himself with the adjective kaved ("heavy, weighty") applied to his organs of speech. This is not mere stammering but a profound self-assessment of verbal incapacity. The same root kaved will later describe the "hardening" of Pharaoh's heart (kavod/kaved -- 7:14, 8:15, 8:32, 9:7, 9:34, 10:1). The wordplay links Moses' self-perceived disability with Pharaoh's willful obstinacy. The LXX reads ischophonos kai braduglossos ("weak-voiced and slow-tongued"), interpreting rather than translating. LOGOS preserves the Hebrew image literally.

6. **"Send, I pray, by the hand of whomever You will send" (v. 13):** Hebrew shelach-na beyad tishlach. This is Moses' fifth and final objection. The phrase is deliberately vague -- "send by the hand of [whoever] You will send" -- a polite refusal veiled as deference. The repetition of the root sh-l-ch (send) underscores the irony: Moses asks God to send someone else, using the very verb of his own commission. Some interpret this as a request for a messianic figure or prophetic successor; at its plain level, it is reluctance. The LXX reads procheirisai allon dunamenon ("appoint another who is able"), making the refusal explicit. LOGOS preserves the Hebrew ambiguity.

7. **"Aaron your brother, the Levite" (v. 14):** The phrase "the Levite" (haLevi) is curious, since Moses himself is also a Levite. Some interpreters suggest it is a title or designation of Aaron's role rather than merely tribal identification. Others propose it functions as a contrast: Moses has been living as a Midianite shepherd, while Aaron has maintained his Levitical identity in Egypt. LOGOS translates directly without resolving the ambiguity.

8. **"He shall be for you as a mouth, and you shall be for him as God" (v. 16):** Hebrew hu yihyeh-lekha lefeh ve'attah tihyeh-lo le'lohim. The analogy is striking and deliberate: Aaron speaks what Moses gives him, as a prophet speaks what God gives him. This prefigures Exodus 7:1 ("I have made you as God to Pharaoh, and Aaron your brother shall be your prophet"). The preposition le ("as/for") preserves the analogical rather than ontological sense. LOGOS renders "as God" to maintain the force of the comparison.

9. **"I will harden his heart" (v. 21):** Hebrew va'ani achazzeq et-libbo. The verb ch-z-q (Piel: "to strengthen, make firm, harden") marks the first divine announcement of Pharaoh's hardening. Throughout Exodus, three different verbs describe this phenomenon: ch-z-q ("strengthen/harden"), k-v-d ("make heavy"), and q-sh-h ("make stiff"). LOGOS translates ch-z-q consistently as "harden" in these contexts, while noting that the Hebrew semantic range includes "strengthen" -- God makes Pharaoh's heart firm in its resolve. The theological tension between divine sovereignty and human agency is preserved, not resolved, by the text.

10. **"Israel is My son, My firstborn" (v. 22):** Hebrew beni bekhori Yisra'el. The declaration of Israel as God's firstborn son is one of the most consequential statements in the Torah. It establishes the theological basis for the tenth plague: Pharaoh refuses to release God's firstborn, so God takes Pharaoh's firstborn. The term bekhor ("firstborn") carries legal weight -- rights of inheritance, special status, double portion. Applied to an entire nation, it declares Israel's unique covenantal relationship with God. The LXX renders huios prototokomou Israel ("my firstborn son, Israel"), preserving the full force.

11. **"The bridegroom of blood" (vv. 24-26):** Hebrew chatan damim. These three verses are among the most enigmatic in the entire Hebrew Bible. The passage is syntactically ambiguous at nearly every point: (a) Who does the LORD seek to kill -- Moses or the son? The pronoun "him" (lo) has no clear antecedent. (b) Whose "feet" (raglav) does Zipporah touch with the foreskin -- Moses' or the child's or the LORD's? The word raglayim ("feet") can be a euphemism for genitals. (c) What does chatan damim mean -- "bridegroom of blood," "blood-relative by circumcision," "circumcision-kinsman"? The word chatan can mean "bridegroom," "son-in-law," or "one who is related by circumcision" (from a root attested in Arabic meaning "to circumcise"). LOGOS preserves the received Hebrew at every point without resolving the ambiguities: "the LORD met him and sought to kill him"; "touched it to his feet"; "a bridegroom of blood to me." The strangeness is the meaning. The passage appears to establish that Moses' son must bear the covenant sign of circumcision before Moses can carry out his mission as God's agent. Zipporah's decisive action saves the life that God threatens. The phrase "He relented from him" (vayyiref mimmennu) uses the same root r-p-h ("to relax, release, let go") found elsewhere for divine withdrawal. The LXX reads "the blood of the circumcision of my child stood" (este to haima tes peritomes tou paidiou mou), a significant interpretive expansion that clarifies the child as the object of circumcision.

12. **"Attended to" (v. 31):** Hebrew paqad, the same verb used in 3:16 ("I have surely attended to you"). This verbal echo closes the loop: God promised Moses that the elders would hear this word (3:18), and now they do. The people's response -- bowing down and worshipping -- validates Moses' commission and fulfills the divine prediction of 3:18 ("they will listen to your voice"). The LXX uses epeskepsato ("visited/attended to"), preserving the link.

13. **"And He relented from him" (v. 26):** Hebrew vayyiref mimmennu. Rendered "He relented" rather than "He let him alone" or "He withdrew." The verb raphah means "to sink, relax, let drop" -- it conveys a releasing of lethal intent. The subject is understood to be the LORD. LOGOS avoids the domesticating "left him alone" in favor of the more theologically charged "relented," which preserves the sense that a divine will was actively redirected.

### Consistent Term Renderings (Exodus Series)
| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| bney Yisra'el | sons of Israel | Not "children of Israel" or "Israelites" |
| Par'oh | Pharaoh | Retained as title |
| YHWH | the LORD | Small-caps convention; represents the Tetragrammaton |
| Elohim | God | When referring to the God of Israel |
| matteh | staff | Shepherd's rod; becomes "staff of God" in v. 20 |
| nachash | serpent | Distinguished from tannin in ch. 7 |
| metsora'at | leprous | Broad skin condition, not only Hansen's disease |
| kaved peh | heavy of mouth | Literal; links to Pharaoh's "heavy" heart |
| chatan damim | bridegroom of blood | Preserved without resolution; vv. 24-26 |
| paqad | attended to | Echoes 3:16; God's caring visitation |
| chazaq (Piel) | harden | First of three hardening verbs (ch-z-q, k-v-d, q-sh-h) |
| bekhor | firstborn | Legal and covenantal term; v. 22 |
| Yitro | Jethro | Moses' father-in-law; = Reuel of 2:18 |
| Tsipporah | Zipporah | Moses' wife; name means "bird" |

— LOGOS
